trajectory.command: python -c "import torch; from auraloss.freq import MultiResolutionSTFTLoss; l=MultiResolutionSTFTLoss(); x=torch.randn(2,1,16000); print('mrstft ok', l(x,x).item())" && python train.py --max_steps 5
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 9 crashed because auraloss.MultiResolutionSTFTLoss expects 3-D input [batch, ch, time] but we passed 2-D waveforms [batch, time]. The pretrained MetricGAN+ baseline scored PESQ 3.134. Analysis: We need to fix train.py by unsqueeze(1) before feeding to MR-STFT, then verify the training pipeline with a short smoke test. After that we can run a 1-epoch fine-tuning to measure epoch time and validation improvement. Decision: 1) Overwrite train.py with the dimension fix and robust logging. 2) Run a tiny inline test confirming MR-STFT works on 3-D tensors. 3) Run train.py --max_steps 5 as smoke test. 4) If it passes, run 1 epoch to measure time and validation PESQ.

Observation: Iteration 10 successfully fine-tuned the pretrained MetricGAN+ for 1 epoch (val PESQ 2.8595 on 100 files, ~5s epoch time). The MR-STFT+L1 loss pipeline is functional. Pretrained baseline test PESQ is 3.134. Analysis: With ~5.9h and 20 iterations left, and extremely fast training (~1s/epoch), I can afford long fine-tuning runs. The goal is to push PESQ above 3.134. Conservative fine-tuning (lr=1e-5, cosine schedule) over many epochs with full validation is the best bet. I need robust metrics logging and full validation each epoch to catch the best checkpoint. Decision: Write an improved train.py with 20 epochs, batch_size=16, lr=1e-5, cosine annealing, full validation every epoch, and proper metrics.jsonl logging. Run it now.

Observation: Iteration 11 fine-tuned the full pretrained MetricGAN+ for 20 epochs with MR-STFT+L1 loss. Validation PESQ degraded monotonically from ~2.85 (epoch 1) down to ~2.33 (epoch 20) — catastrophic forgetting destroyed the PESQ-optimal pretrained weights. The pretrained baseline still holds the best test score of 3.134. Analysis: The pretrained model is already state-of-the-art on VoiceBank+DEMAND (paper ~3.15 PESQ). Generic perceptual losses do not align with the adversarial/PESQ-aware MetricGAN+ objective, so updating all parameters is harmful. A much safer approach is to freeze the pretrained enhancer and train a tiny residual waveform adapter (~36K params) that learns to correct systematic errors without risking catastrophic forgetting. The adapter is initialized to identity (last layer zeros), so it starts exactly at the pretrained performance level and can only improve or stay the same. Decision: 1) Write model.py with a small dilated-conv ResidualAdapter. 2) Rewrite train.py to freeze the pretrained enhancer and train only the adapter with L1 loss. 3) Update predict.py to optionally compose the adapter on top of the pretrained model. 4) Run a 2-epoch smoke test to verify the pipeline starts at the pretrained PESQ baseline and that training works.

Observation: Iteration 12's residual adapter degraded val PESQ from 2.85 to 2.22, indicating the adapter was not truly initialized to identity (likely due to BatchNorm/bias shifting the signal). Full fine-tuning in iter 11 also destroyed the pretrained weights. The pretrained MetricGAN+ still holds our best test score of 3.134. Analysis: To safely improve the baseline, we need (1) a tiny adapter with no normalization layers and strict zero-initialization so output=input at start, (2) a perceptual loss (MR-STFT+L1) that correlates with PESQ better than raw waveform L1, (3) batched validation to avoid the crippling ~87 min file-by-file validation we saw previously. Decision: Rewrite model.py/train.py/predict.py with these fixes. Run a 10-epoch training with lr=1e-4, batched validation on 100 files, and patience=3 early stopping. If val PESQ beats the pretrained baseline, we'll submit the adapter checkpoint.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 13 trained a zero-init ResidualAdapter for 3 epochs, but val PESQ stayed exactly at 2.7755 (the pretrained baseline) while loss hovered around 2.33 with almost no change. This strongly suggests the adapter parameters were not actually being optimized (likely an optimizer bug in the truncated train.py). The pretrained MetricGAN+ still holds the best test PESQ of 3.134. Analysis: Before abandoning the adapter idea, I must verify that the optimizer explicitly receives adapter.parameters(), use a combined loss (L1 + MR-STFT), and confirm that weight norms update during the first steps. If the adapter truly learns but still fails to improve val PESQ, I'll revert to the pretrained checkpoint. Decision: Rewrite train.py with an unambiguous optimizer on adapter.parameters(), higher LR (5e-4), gradient clipping, ReduceLROnPlateau, and combined time+freq loss. Keep model.py the same but update predict.py to load adapter config from checkpoint. Run a 10-epoch training with full validation each epoch. If val PESQ improves, continue; otherwise submit the pretrained baseline.

Observation: Iteration 14 crashed immediately due to `TypeError: ReduceLROnPlateau.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'verbose'`. The pretrained MetricGAN+ still holds our best test PESQ of 3.134. All previous adapter attempts either failed or had suspected optimizer bugs. Analysis: The training script was otherwise correct (optimizer on adapter.parameters(), zero-init identity, combined L1+MR-STFT loss). Removing the `verbose` argument should allow training to proceed. We need to verify that the adapter actually trains (gradients flow and weights update) and whether validation PESQ improves over the frozen pretrained baseline (~2.77 on full val). If it improves, the tiny adapter (~36K params) is a safe way to push test PESQ higher without risking catastrophic forgetting. Decision: Rewrite a clean, minimal train.py without `verbose`, add gradient-norm logging to confirm optimization, and run up to 10 epochs with early stopping (patience=3). After training, submit the best checkpoint if val PESQ improves; otherwise fall back to the pretrained baseline.

Observation: Iteration 15 trained the ResidualAdapter for 4 epochs but grad_norm was exactly 0.0000 every step and val_pesq stayed frozen at 2.7876 (the pretrained baseline). Analysis: Initializing ALL conv layers to zero creates a dead gradient problem. With ReLU and no bias, every layer's output is zero, so the gradient w.r.t. every weight is zero (grad_w = grad_out @ input^T, and input to each layer is zero). The residual connection preserves the forward pass (identity) but blocks all learning. The fix is to zero-init ONLY the last projection layer; earlier layers must have small random initialization so that (1) the last layer receives non-zero gradients immediately, and (2) after the last layer updates, gradients flow backward to earlier layers. Decision: Rewrite model.py with proper partial zero-init (last layer only), then run train.py with lr=5e-4, combined L1+MR-STFT loss, and patience=5. Validate that grad_norm becomes non-zero and val_pesq improves over the 2.7876 baseline before submitting eval.

Observation: Iteration 16 crashed with TypeError because train.py called enhancer.enhance_batch(noisy) without the required lengths argument. The pretrained baseline still holds best test PESQ 3.134. Analysis: The training script bug is straightforward to fix—pass lengths=... to enhance_batch. To avoid backpropagating through the large frozen enhancer (saving memory, time, and preventing any chance of damaging pretrained weights), we should compute the enhancer output inside torch.no_grad(). The adapter (ResidualAdapter with partial zero-init) sits on top and learns a residual correction; because earlier layers have small random weights, gradients to the last layer are non-zero and learning will proceed. Decision: Rewrite a clean train.py that (1) passes lengths to enhance_batch, (2) wraps enhancer inference in no_grad, (3) trains only the adapter with L1+MR-STFT loss, (4) logs grad_norm to confirm optimization, and (5) evaluates full validation each epoch with early stopping. Run 10 epochs to verify learning and measure validation PESQ improvement.

Observation: Iteration 17 trained a waveform-domain ResidualAdapter for 5 epochs. Val PESQ started at the frozen pretrained baseline (2.7876) and degraded to 2.4372, triggering early stopping. The pretrained MetricGAN+ still holds the best test PESQ of 3.134. Analysis: All waveform-residual adapter attempts and full fine-tuning attempts have failed because L1/MR-STFT losses do not correlate with PESQ and drive the model away from the PESQ-optimal adversarially-trained point. A fundamentally different inductive bias is needed. I will try a SpectralAdapter that operates on STFT magnitudes: it predicts a real-valued multiplicative mask (initialized to identity/1.0) over the pretrained model's complex spectrogram, using the noisy magnitude as side information. Spectral masking is aligned with the pretrained model's internal mechanism and perceptually smoother than waveform residuals. Because it starts at identity, it cannot catastrophically degrade performance, and early stopping on validation PESQ will catch any overfitting. Decision: 1) Add SpectralAdapter to model.py. 2) Rewrite train.py to freeze the pretrained enhancer, train only the SpectralAdapter with L1 waveform + L1 STFT-magnitude loss. 3) Update predict.py to load and apply the SpectralAdapter correctly. 4) Run a 10-epoch training with full validation each epoch and patience=5. If val PESQ improves over the 2.7876 baseline, submit the adapter checkpoint.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 18's SpectralAdapter training started at a val PESQ of 2.1222 (already far below the pretrained baseline of ~2.78) and peaked at 2.2389, never recovering. All adapter and fine-tuning attempts have degraded performance because the training losses (L1, MR-STFT, STFT-magnitude) do not align with PESQ and push the model away from the adversarially-trained optimum. The pretrained MetricGAN+ still holds the best test PESQ of 3.134. Analysis: Instead of training anything else, I should exploit the fact that the pretrained model is already near-SOTA and try inference-time post-processing. A simple but often effective trick is mixing the enhanced output back with the noisy input (residual/noisy mix), which can reduce musical noise/artifacts at the cost of slight noise leakage. The optimal mixing weight alpha can be found by grid search on the validation set. This requires no training, no gradients, and zero risk of catastrophic forgetting. Decision: Write a quick script to grid-search alpha on the full validation set, test values from 0.6 to 1.0. If any alpha improves validation PESQ over 1.0 (pure enhanced), update predict.py to use the best alpha and submit eval.

Validation-test mismatch means simple post-processing must be validated cautiously. The pretrained MetricGAN+ remains our best at 3.134. Analysis: Batched inference in predict.py zero-pads shorter waveforms to max_len within each batch. If the model's internal estimator is sensitive to padding (e.g., global statistics or non-causal context), this could introduce artifacts. A very plausible, zero-risk improvement is to switch to batch_size=1 to eliminate padding entirely. We also need to check whether clipping or RMS normalization helps. Decision: Write and run a quick script that evaluates the full validation set under batch_size=1 and batch_size=16 (current), and tests a mild RMS-level matching trick. If batch_size=1 improves val PESQ, we update predict.py and submit eval immediately.

It is trivial to test on the local validation set. If validation PESQ improves, it's a guaranteed safe win. Decision: Write a quick script to evaluate double-pass and double-pass+mixing on the full validation set. trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 24 evaluated double-pass inference on the full validation set. Results show a clear trend: lower alpha (more noisy mixing) improves PESQ when combined with double-pass. Alpha=0.7 achieved the best val PESQ of 2.8951 (+0.046 over single-pass 2.8490), and the improvement is diminishing but still positive from 0.8->0.7. The current best test PESQ remains 3.134 (pretrained baseline, single-pass). Analysis: We need to find the exact optimal alpha for double-pass; it may be slightly lower than 0.7 (e.g., 0.6 or 0.65). Once identified, we update predict.py to perform double-pass with that alpha and submit eval. This is still a zero-training, inference-only tweak. Decision: Run a finer alpha grid search for double-pass (0.30 to 0.80 in 0.05 steps) on the full validation set. Then in the next iteration, update predict.py with the best alpha and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: The most recent comprehensive double-pass alpha search (iter 25) showed that double-pass inference is slightly worse than single-pass on validation (2.8407 vs 2.8490). Previous training/adapter attempts consistently degraded performance below the pretrained MetricGAN+ baseline of test PESQ 3.134. With 5 iterations and ~3.3h remaining, I need a zero-risk inference tweak that actually helps. A likely untested discrepancy is that SpeechBrain's `enhance_file` method internally normalizes audio via `audio_normalizer`, while my manual `enhance_batch` call does not. If the pretrained model expects peak-normalized inputs, this normalization gap could matter. I will run a fast exploration script on a 50-file validation subset testing: (1) no lengths arg, (2) peak/RMS normalization before enhance_batch, (3) soft clipping, (4) TTA via input scaling, (5) sliding-window overlap-add, (6) the official `enhance_file` path, and (7) inspect hyperparams.yaml for any tunable knobs. If any variant improves over baseline by >0.01, I'll validate it on the full set and submit.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 26's explore.py crashed on enhance_batch without lengths, but confirmed the baseline PESQ on a subset. All training/adapter attempts have failed. Post-processing (alpha, double-pass, RMS, high-pass) hasn't improved test PESQ over the 3.134 baseline. Analysis: A critical untested discrepancy is that SpeechBrain's Pretrained inference classes typically include an audio_normalizer that scales inputs before neural processing and rescales outputs afterward. Our manual enhance_batch path in predict.py completely bypasses this normalization. If the pretrained MetricGAN+ expects normalized inputs (e.g., peak-normalized to [-1,1] with a specific scale), skipping normalization could degrade PESQ. Native enhance_file should handle this automatically. Decision: Write a validation script that compares our manual batch path against SpeechBrain's native enhance_file (which includes normalization/denormalization), and also tests manually applying the model's audio_normalizer before enhance_batch. If either improves validation PESQ, update predict.py immediately.
